content.text: Business Sign-Off and UAT Acceptance Criteria

UAT produces a technical recommendation, but the business sign-off process converts that recommendation into a formal release authorization. In an enterprise context, a sign-off is both a quality gate and an accountability record — it establishes who evaluated what evidence, what risk level was accepted, and who is responsible if post-launch problems emerge. For Claude-powered features, this process requires additional structure because AI behavior is harder to specify than traditional software, and business stakeholders often lack the vocabulary to evaluate LLM quality reports without translation.

A business sign-off is not approval that the software is perfect. It is documented agreement that the evidence meets the acceptance criteria, that residual risks are understood and owned, and that the team is prepared to respond if post-launch monitoring surfaces unexpected behavior.

This sub-subtopic covers how to define acceptance criteria that business stakeholders can evaluate, build the stakeholder sign-off matrix, present UAT evidence in business terms, manage conditional approvals with waivers, and archive the sign-off record for compliance and audit purposes.

attrs.alt: Business Sign-Off Process for Claude Feature Releases

content.text: Translating UAT Results into Business Criteria

Technical UAT metrics (pass rate, latency percentile, judge scores) are not directly readable by business stakeholders. The sign-off process requires a translation layer that maps each technical result to a business outcome the stakeholder is accountable for.

# acceptance_criteria.py — business-facing acceptance criteria definition
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class RiskLevel(str, Enum):
    BLOCKING = "blocking"      # must pass before any release
    HIGH = "high"              # must pass for full rollout
    MEDIUM = "medium"          # must pass for broad rollout; waivable for limited
    LOW = "low"                # advisory; tracked but not blocking

@dataclass
class BusinessCriterion:
    id: str
    title: str                   # stakeholder-readable name
    business_rationale: str      # why this matters in business terms
    technical_measurement: str   # the UAT metric that proves this
    threshold: str               # the passing bar (human-readable)
    risk_level: RiskLevel
    owner: str                   # business stakeholder accountable for this criterion
    waivable: bool = False       # can this be waived with documented risk acceptance?

# Example criteria set for a customer support Claude feature
SUPPORT_CHAT_CRITERIA = [
    BusinessCriterion(
        id="bc-01",
        title="Scope containment",
        business_rationale="Claude must not answer questions outside billing and account management to prevent liability from incorrect financial or legal advice.",
        technical_measurement="Boundary handling scenario pass rate (automated classifier)",
        threshold="100% — no out-of-scope responses in any tested scenario",
        risk_level=RiskLevel.BLOCKING,
        owner="Head of Legal",
        waivable=False,
    ),
    BusinessCriterion(
        id="bc-02",
        title="Customer satisfaction projection",
        business_rationale="Claude must maintain or improve current CSAT scores to avoid churn impact.",
        technical_measurement="End-user tester survey: helpfulness + trust (5-point Likert)",
        threshold="Average ≥ 4.0/5.0 across both dimensions",
        risk_level=RiskLevel.HIGH,
        owner="VP Customer Experience",
        waivable=True,
    ),
    BusinessCriterion(
        id="bc-03",
        title="Response accuracy",
        business_rationale="Incorrect answers about account policies create customer frustration and support escalations.",
        technical_measurement="Domain expert review: factual accuracy on policy questions",
        threshold="≥ 90% accuracy across SME-evaluated sample of 20 responses",
        risk_level=RiskLevel.HIGH,
        owner="Director of Support Operations",
        waivable=False,
    ),
    BusinessCriterion(
        id="bc-04",
        title="Response latency",
        business_rationale="Latency above 3 seconds significantly degrades chat experience and increases abandonment.",
        technical_measurement="p95 end-to-end response latency in load test",
        threshold="p95 ≤ 3 seconds under expected peak load (500 concurrent users)",
        risk_level=RiskLevel.MEDIUM,
        owner="Head of Engineering",
        waivable=True,
    ),
    BusinessCriterion(
        id="bc-05",
        title="Harmful output prevention",
        business_rationale="Any response that could cause user harm — incorrect emergency advice, discriminatory language — is unacceptable and creates legal exposure.",
        technical_measurement="Safety scenario pass rate (red-team scenarios + automated judge)",
        threshold="100% — zero harmful outputs in any tested scenario",
        risk_level=RiskLevel.BLOCKING,
        owner="Chief Risk Officer",
        waivable=False,
    ),
]

Each criterion is owned by a specific business stakeholder who understands the underlying business risk and is authorized to accept it. The mapping makes clear that sign-off is distributed — no single person approves all criteria, and a blocking criterion failure cannot be overridden by someone who does not own it.

The sign-off package is the document presented to stakeholders at the review meeting. It is not a dump of technical metrics — it is a structured narrative that maps each business criterion to the evidence that it has been met (or explains why it has not been met and what waiver is requested).
A common mistake is including raw judge scores or pass rates without explaining what they mean. Each evidence summary in the sign-off package must connect a number to a business implication.

Instead of: 'Boundary handling pass rate: 98.7%' → write: 'In 150 test interactions where users asked about topics outside billing (legal, medical, competitor pricing), Claude redirected correctly in 148 cases. The two failures involved ambiguous billing-vs-legal questions and have been added to the system prompt with explicit handling instructions.'

Instead of: 'p95 latency: 2.8s' → write: 'Under simulated peak load of 500 concurrent users, 95% of responses completed within 2.8 seconds. This is below the 3-second threshold above which user drop-off rates measurably increase based on industry benchmarks.'

Instead of: 'LLM judge accuracy score: 0.87' → write: 'A domain expert reviewed 20 randomly sampled responses to questions about account policies. 18 were rated fully accurate, 1 was partially accurate (answered the question but omitted a relevant caveat), and 1 contained a minor factual error about the refund window (since corrected in the system prompt).'

Not every failure should block a release. A criterion that fails narrowly, on a scenario that affects a small user segment, with a low-severity consequence, may be waivable with appropriate risk documentation and monitoring commitments. The waiver process converts informal 'just ship it' decisions into documented risk acceptance.
Only the criterion owner can approve a waiver for that criterion — a different stakeholder's approval is not valid.
BLOCKING criteria (scope containment, harm prevention) cannot be waived under any circumstances.
Each waiver must include a review date not more than 30 days after launch. The open issue must be resolved or the waiver explicitly renewed.
Waivers are stored in a persistent register (not just the PR description) and referenced in future UAT cycles.
More than two waivers on a single release is a signal that the feature is not ready — escalate rather than stack waivers.

The sign-off review meeting is a structured 60-minute session where stakeholders review the sign-off package and reach the release decision. It is not a status update — it requires stakeholders to actively engage with evidence and confirm their criterion's verdict.

Business Sign-Off Review — [Feature Name] [Release ID]
Duration: 60 minutes
Attendees: All criterion owners + engineering lead + product owner

0:00 – 0:05  | Context (product owner)
              | What does this feature do? Who does it serve?
              | What user problem does it solve?

0:05 – 0:30  | Evidence walkthrough (engineering lead)
              | Walk through each criterion result in the sign-off package.
              | For each PASS: confirm the evidence is sufficient.
              | For each FAIL: explain root cause and remediation plan.
              | For each WAIVER_REQUESTED: present the waiver document.

0:30 – 0:45  | Q&A and deliberation
              | Stakeholders ask questions about evidence they find insufficient.
              | Engineering answers questions about technical measurements.
              | Waiver requests discussed and voted on by criterion owners.

0:45 – 0:55  | Decision
              | Each criterion owner states APPROVE / HOLD / WAIVE for their criterion.
              | Overall decision: APPROVE if all blocking pass + all waivers signed.
              |                   HOLD if any blocking failure.
              |                   CONDITIONAL if waivers pending signature.

0:55 – 1:00  | Next steps
              | If HOLD: assign owners + target date for each failing criterion.
              | If APPROVE/CONDITIONAL: rollout scope, monitoring review date.

The sign-off record is the permanent artifact that documents what was decided, by whom, with what evidence. It serves three purposes: it enables rollback decision-making by documenting what was known at release time; it feeds future UAT by capturing which criteria caused friction; and it satisfies compliance and audit requirements for regulated industries.

# signoff_record.py — archived sign-off record
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class StakeholderApproval:
    name: str
    role: str
    criterion_ids: list[str]  # which criteria they approved
    decision: str             # APPROVE | HOLD | WAIVE
    conditions: str           # any conditions attached to approval
    timestamp: datetime

@dataclass
class SignOffRecord:
    record_id: str
    feature: str
    release: str
    decision: str             # APPROVE | CONDITIONAL | HOLD
    approvals: list[StakeholderApproval] = field(default_factory=list)
    waivers: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # waiver IDs
    rollout_scope: str = ""
    post_launch_review_date: str = ""
    notes: str = ""

    def is_complete(self) -> bool:
        return all(a.decision in ("APPROVE", "WAIVE") for a in self.approvals)

    def to_json(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "record_id": self.record_id,
            "feature": self.feature,
            "release": self.release,
            "decision": self.decision,
            "approvals": [
                {
                    "name": a.name,
                    "role": a.role,
                    "criterion_ids": a.criterion_ids,
                    "decision": a.decision,
                    "conditions": a.conditions,
                    "timestamp": a.timestamp.isoformat(),
                }
                for a in self.approvals
            ],
            "waivers": self.waivers,
            "rollout_scope": self.rollout_scope,
            "post_launch_review_date": self.post_launch_review_date,
            "notes": self.notes,
        }

# archive_signoff.py — persist sign-off record for compliance
import json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime

ARCHIVE_DIR = Path("compliance/signoffs")

def archive_signoff(record: "SignOffRecord") -> Path:
    ARCHIVE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    filename = f"{record.release}-{record.record_id}.json"
    path = ARCHIVE_DIR / filename
    data = record.to_json()
    data["archived_at"] = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
    path.write_text(json.dumps(data, indent=2))
    return path

def generate_audit_summary(start_date: str, end_date: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Pull all sign-off records for an audit period."""
    records = []
    for path in sorted(ARCHIVE_DIR.glob("*.json")):
        data = json.loads(path.read_text())
        if start_date <= data.get("archived_at", "")[:10] <= end_date:
            records.append({
                "release": data["release"],
                "decision": data["decision"],
                "waivers_count": len(data.get("waivers", [])),
                "approvers": [a["name"] for a in data.get("approvals", [])],
                "rollout_scope": data.get("rollout_scope", ""),
            })
    return records

Business sign-off does not end at release. The sign-off record commits the team to a post-launch review at the date specified, where actual production metrics are compared against the projections made in the UAT evidence. This closes the feedback loop: if projections were wrong, the UAT process is improved; if waivers produced the predicted risk, they are resolved; if unexpected issues emerged, they are traced back to gaps in the scenario library.
Scenario gaps, false passes, added scenarios

Every post-launch issue that was not caught by UAT is a debt. The retrospective converts that debt into an asset: new scenarios, tighter criteria, or revised thresholds that make the next cycle more protective.

Business sign-off for Claude features is a structured process that translates UAT results into formal release authorization, with documented accountability for each category of risk. The key elements: business acceptance criteria owned by named stakeholders who understand the underlying risk, evidence packages that present technical results in business language, a waiver process for conditional approvals with documented risk acceptance and monitoring commitments, a structured review meeting where criterion owners make explicit decisions, and a persistent sign-off record that enables audit, rollback decision-making, and UAT improvement.
Acceptance criteria: each maps a business risk to a measurable UAT threshold, with a named owner and risk level.
Sign-off package: translates technical results into business-language evidence summaries.
Waivers: formalize conditional approvals; BLOCKING criteria cannot be waived.
Review meeting: 60-minute structured session with explicit per-criterion decisions.
Archival: persistent compliance record enables audit and feeds post-launch reconciliation.
Post-launch review: 30-day KPI check and UAT retrospective close the feedback loop.
